meta.one_liner: Chronic IL-23/IL-17-driven skin disease; a mature, biologics-led market — the competitive question has shifted from clearing skin to reaching the patient first, at what net price.
meta.sales_note: Franchise-sales donut is company-reported FY2025 totals across ALL indications. The dedicated attribution region below estimates the psoriasis-only split. PASI figures are cross-trial (NMA-anchored) unless a head-to-head is noted.

epidemiology.headline: ~125M worldwide · ~7.5M US adults · ~20% moderate-to-severe
epidemiology.comorbidities: Elevated cardiovascular, metabolic-syndrome, IBD and depression risk; psoriatic arthritis in ~30%.
journey: Step-up to oral systemics / biologics for moderate-to-severe
Biologic maintenance; switching on loss of response / comorbidity
epidemiology.unmet: Large share of moderate-to-severe patients remain untreated or under-treated (cost, access, injection aversion). Durable drug-free remission and hard-to-treat sites remain the key gaps.
epidemiology.dlqi: Mean DLQI ~8–12 in moderate-to-severe disease — a large quality-of-life impact, comparable to that of cancer or heart disease.
epidemiology.economic: US direct + indirect burden estimated >$100B/yr, roughly half from lost work productivity and comorbidity care.
epidemiology.drug_survival: Real-world persistence is highest for IL-23 inhibitors (~1-yr drug survival ~85–90%) and lowest for TNF inhibitors — a key durability differentiator beyond trial PASI.
epidemiology.mortality: Not directly fatal, but severe psoriasis carries excess cardiovascular mortality — the basis for treat-to-target inflammation control.

sites.note: High-visibility, itch-driven; foams/solutions; a common driver of systemic step-up.
Slow to respond; often needs systemic therapy; predicts psoriatic arthritis.
Painful, disabling, frequently refractory to standard therapy.
Major QoL impact; sensitive-site tolerability; historically understudied.
Growing label set (etanercept, ustekinumab, ixekizumab, secukinumab, apremilast, icotrokinra ≥12).

lines.label: Topical therapy
lines.note: First line for mild / localised disease (~80% of patients). Steroid-sparing non-steroidals now extend to face, folds and scalp.

lines.label: Phototherapy
lines.note: Moderate disease or topical-refractory; office- or home-based. Safe in pregnancy; limited by access and time burden.

lines.label: Conventional systemics
lines.note: Moderate-to-severe; low-cost and widely used globally. Requires organ-toxicity monitoring; cyclosporine for short bursts.

lines.label: Advanced oral
lines.note: Oral targeted therapy — the fastest-growing tier; increasingly a first-line systemic choice for oral-preferring patients.

lines.label: Biologics
lines.note: Moderate-to-severe; the deepest, most durable clearance (PASI 90–100). IL-23 and IL-17 are the modern anchors.

standard_of_care.note: Escalation is not strictly stepwise — moderate-to-severe patients increasingly start advanced oral or biologic therapy directly. Basis: AAD-NPF guidelines of care.

moa_landscape.safety: Cleanest profile — no increased candidiasis or IBD signal (may be IBD-protective), low serious-infection rates, no boxed warnings.
Mucocutaneous Candida (highest with bimekizumab); caution/avoid in IBD; brodalumab carries a boxed warning for suicidal ideation. No consistent MACE/malignancy signal.
Boxed warnings: serious infections (incl. TB reactivation — screen first) and malignancy (lymphoma). HBV reactivation, demyelination, CHF worsening; highest herpes-zoster rate.

Selective TYK2 (deucravacitinib) does NOT carry the JAK boxed warning; 5-yr data show no MACE/VTE/malignancy signal. Classic JAK inhibitors carry the class boxed warning (infection, MACE, thrombosis, malignancy).
No boxed warning, no lab monitoring. GI (diarrhea/nausea), weight loss, and a depression/mood warning (apremilast).
Local, well-tolerated; steroid-sparing. Suitable for chronic use and sensitive sites.
Skin atrophy, striae, tachyphylaxis and HPA-axis suppression with chronic/high-potency use.
Organ toxicity and monitoring: MTX (hepatotoxicity, cytopenia, teratogenic), cyclosporine (nephrotoxicity, hypertension), acitretin (teratogenic, hyperlipidemia).

nodes.label: Dendritic cell
nodes.description: Dermal antigen-presenting cell. On activation it secretes IL-23, IL-12 and TNF, initiating and sustaining the pathogenic T-cell response that drives psoriatic plaques.

nodes.label: Th17 cell
nodes.description: IL-23-dependent effector T cell. It signals intracellularly through TYK2 / JAK-STAT and secretes the effector cytokines IL-17A, IL-17F and IL-22.

nodes.label: Keratinocyte
nodes.description: Epidermal cell. Under IL-17 and TNF it hyperproliferates and releases further chemokines and cytokines, closing a self-amplifying inflammatory loop that forms the scaly plaque.

nodes.label: Psoriatic plaque
nodes.description: The clinical lesion — a well-demarcated, thickened, scaly erythematous plaque produced by keratinocyte hyperproliferation and immune infiltration.

nodes.label: IL-23
nodes.description: Dendritic-cell cytokine (p19 + p40) that maintains pathogenic Th17 cells — the most disease-specific and best-validated target in psoriasis.

nodes.label: IL-12/23 p40
nodes.description: Shared subunit of IL-12 and IL-23. Blocking p40 (ustekinumab) inhibits both; selective p19 blockade has since proven more effective.

nodes.label: IL-17A / IL-17F
nodes.description: The effector cytokines acting on keratinocytes. Neutralising them delivers the fastest, deepest skin clearance of any class.

nodes.label: IL-22
nodes.description: Th17-derived cytokine promoting keratinocyte proliferation and epidermal acanthosis.

nodes.label: TNF-α
nodes.description: Pleiotropic pro-inflammatory cytokine that amplifies the cascade at multiple points; the first successful biologic target in psoriasis.

nodes.label: TYK2
nodes.description: Intracellular pseudokinase that transduces IL-23 (and type-I interferon) signalling — the basis of oral allosteric TYK2 inhibition.

nodes.label: PDE4 / cAMP
nodes.description: Phosphodiesterase-4 regulates intracellular cAMP in immune cells; inhibiting it dampens pro-inflammatory cytokine output (apremilast, roflumilast).

nodes.label: IL-23 receptor
nodes.description: Cell-surface receptor complex (IL-23R + IL-12Rβ1) on Th17 cells. Engaged by IL-23; the target of the first oral IL-23R peptide antagonist (icotrokinra).

nodes.label: IL-17RA receptor
nodes.description: Keratinocyte receptor (IL-17RA/RC) for IL-17A/F. Neutralised directly by brodalumab; signals via Act1 to NF-κB.

nodes.label: JAK2
nodes.description: Janus kinase paired with TYK2 beneath the IL-23 receptor; together they phosphorylate STAT3 on cytokine binding.

nodes.label: STAT3
nodes.description: Transcription factor phosphorylated by TYK2/JAK2. It translocates to the nucleus and switches on the Th17 genetic programme.

nodes.label: RORγt
nodes.description: Master transcription factor of Th17 identity, induced downstream of STAT3; it drives IL-17/IL-22 production and is an emerging small-molecule target.

nodes.label: NF-κB
nodes.description: Central inflammatory transcription factor activated in keratinocytes by IL-17 and TNF, amplifying chemokine and cytokine output.

ch.d: Selective p19 blockade starves pathogenic Th17 cells — the deepest, most durable maintenance class.
Blocks the shared p40 subunit (IL-12 + IL-23); first-generation, now facing biosimilars.
First targeted oral peptide blocking the IL-23 receptor — biologic-like selectivity in a pill.
Neutralise the IL-23 signal that sustains Th17 cells — the most disease-specific axis.
Neutralise the dominant effector cytokine; fast, deep clearance.
Dual IL-17A + IL-17F blockade — the deepest PASI-100 rates to date.
Blocks the receptor (all IL-17 ligands); boxed warning shaped uptake.
Affibody and oral small-molecule attempts to bring IL-17 blockade to a pill.
Neutralise the effector cytokines acting on keratinocytes.
The original biologic target; now biosimilar-eroded but still workhorses, esp. with joint disease.
Neutralise a specific cytokine or receptor with an engineered protein — the deepest, most durable clearance.
Allosteric TYK2 blockade avoids the JAK boxed warning; the fast-growing oral class.
Broader kinase inhibition (incl. topical JAK); efficacy vs class safety trade-offs.
Inhibit the intracellular signalling that transduces IL-23 and other cytokines.
Dampen cytokine output; modest efficacy, benign safety, no monitoring.
Convenient oral therapy — increasingly a first-line systemic choice.
Steroid-sparing keratinocyte-normalising agents (AhR, vitamin-D, retinoid, PDE4) now reaching scalp & folds.
Anti-inflammatory mainstay for mild / localised disease.
First line for mild or localised disease (~80% of patients).
Low-cost, widely used globally; require organ-toxicity monitoring.
Cost-effective moderate-to-severe therapy where biologics are inaccessible.

strategy_map.d: Psoriasis is a chronic IL-23/IL-17-driven disease; the dominant goal is durable skin clearance (PASI 90–100), escalating from topicals to cytokine blockade and oral targeted agents.

ch.d: A distinct IL-36-driven neutrophilic variant; spesolimab is the first approved GPP-specific therapy.
A severe, distinct pustular variant driven by the IL-36 axis rather than IL-23/IL-17.
Scalp, nails, palmoplantar, genital and pediatric disease drive systemic step-up. No unique mechanism — the battleground is label expansion of IL-17 / IL-23 agents (see the sites panel).
Special sites and pediatrics carry outsized QoL impact and shape switching.

strategy_map.d: Beyond plaque clearance, distinct variants and hard-to-treat sites need dedicated mechanisms and label expansion.

ch.d: IL-22, OSMRβ/IL-31 and GM-CSF — exploratory effectors beyond the IL-23/IL-17 core.
RIPK1 and A3-adenosine programmes probing new inflammatory nodes (efficacy so far modest).
Exploratory mechanisms seeking efficacy or durability the core axes don't deliver.
S1P modulation and (withdrawn) T-cell-directed agents — the historical arc toward immune modulation and, aspirationally, drug-free remission.
The whitespace: durable, treatment-free remission remains unproven — the field's biggest unmet goal.

strategy_map.d: No therapy yet delivers durable drug-free remission; this is the field's aspirational frontier, sparsely populated and high-risk.

response_kinetics.note: Class-representative PASI-90 trajectories (NMA / onset-of-action-informed; illustrative). IL-17 acts fastest and deepest; IL-23 p19 climbs more slowly but is durable; oral agents plateau lower.

landmark_trials.result: First IL-17A pivotal; PASI 90 ~59%, superior to etanercept.
IL-17RA blockade; very high clearance; a boxed warning later shaped uptake.
PASI 90 ~68–71% — among the fastest, deepest responders of its era.
IL-23 p19; PASI 90 ~73% at wk16, superior to adalimumab.
IL-23 p19; established q12w dosing; PASI 90 ~35–39%.
PASI 90 ~75%, superior to ustekinumab — set the IL-23 benchmark.
Head-to-head win: guselkumab beat secukinumab on wk-48 PASI 90 (durability).

landmark_trials.trial: BE VIVID / BE READY / BE SURE
landmark_trials.result: Dual IL-17A/F; PASI 100 ~60% — the deepest-clearance benchmark to date.
First oral TYK2; PASI 75 ~58% vs apremilast ~35% — opened the oral-targeted era.
Next-gen oral TYK2: >50% PASI 90 — raising the oral efficacy bar.
First oral IL-23R peptide; positioned as a first-line systemic.

trials.name: ICONIC-LEAD
trials.readout: Positive topline (2025): ~65% IGA 0/1 and ~50–65% PASI 90 at Week 16 — first oral to approach injectable IL-23 efficacy.
trials.inclusion: Adults & adolescents ≥12 y; moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis; candidate for systemic therapy or phototherapy.
trials.sites: Global, multi-country (part of the 6-study ICONIC Phase 3 programme)
trials.kols: Industry-sponsored; site investigators listed on ClinicalTrials.gov
trials.significance: Category-defining first-line ORAL IL-23R peptide — the pill that rivals a biologic.

trials.name: Zasocitinib Phase 3 (TAK-279)
trials.readout: >50% PASI 90 and ~30% PASI 100 at Week 16 — best-in-class oral efficacy for a TYK2 inhibitor.
trials.inclusion: Adults ≥18 y; moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis (BSA ≥10%, PASI ≥12, sPGA ≥3).
trials.significance: Next-gen oral TYK2 raising the oral bar toward biologic-level clearance.

trials.name: ONWARD (ESK-001)
trials.readout: ~74% PASI 75 at Week 16; NDA planned H2 2026 — a competitive oral TYK2.
trials.inclusion: Adults ≥18 y; moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis; systemic-therapy candidate.
trials.significance: BID oral TYK2 competing on depth and durability with deucravacitinib and zasocitinib.

trials.name: BE VIVID / BE RADIANT (bimekizumab)
trials.readout: ~60% PASI 100 at Week 16 with high maintenance — the deepest, most durable clearance to date.
trials.sites: Global; BE-series pivotal trials + open-label extensions
trials.significance: Sets the PASI 100 ceiling — the efficacy benchmark competitors are measured against.

trials.name: Sonelokimab Phase 2 (M-1095 / MIRA)
trials.status: PsO Ph2 positive; sponsor prioritising HS
trials.readout: ~80% PASI 90 in Ph2 with a small IL-17A/F Nanobody; PsO development deprioritised in favour of hidradenitis.
trials.significance: Nanobody format tests whether a small IL-17A/F protein can match mAbs — a platform bet.

trials.name: POETYK PSO long-term extension
endpoints: Maintenance of PASI 75 / sPGA 0/1 over time
trials.readout: Durable response and a clean multi-year safety profile — supporting first-oral-TYK2 positioning.
trials.inclusion: POETYK PSO-1/-2 completers; moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis.
trials.significance: Durability + safety are the oral-TYK2 differentiator versus classic JAK inhibitors.

trials_focus.note: The most strategically important programmes. Endpoints, eligibility, sites and dates would be pulled live from ClinicalTrials.gov (API v2) by NCT id; figures here are curated from cited toplines. Named KOLs are pivotal-trial lead authors; industry site investigators are listed on ClinicalTrials.gov.

catalysts.significance: First big psoriasis-biologic patent cliff; ~9 biosimilars in 2023.
First IL-17A and IL-17F inhibitor; deepest skin-clearance benchmark.
~7 biosimilars launch in 2025 at up to ~85% discount; Stelara ~$10.4B (2024).

catalysts.event: Zoryve (roflumilast) foam approved for scalp & body
catalysts.significance: Extends the topical PDE4 franchise into scalp.
>50% PASI 90, ~30% PASI 100 at wk16; positions oral TYK2 as a 2026 filer.
~74% PASI 75; oral TYK2 competitor; NDA planned H2 2026.
First targeted ORAL IL-23R peptide; category-defining first-line oral.
Longest runway of the IL-23 class; AbbVie's post-Humira anchor.

deals.note: Deucravacitinib (oral TYK2, then BMS-986165) entered BMS via the $74B Celgene acquisition.
Otezla (PDE4) was divested for antitrust clearance during the BMS–Celgene merger.
Allosteric oral TYK2 TAK-279 acquired from Nimbus after strong Ph2b.
Oral IL-17 antagonist platform (DELSCAPE) acquired for early oral biologics-alternatives.
Topical AhR agonist Vtama acquired to build Organon's dermatology franchise.
Merged next-gen oral-immunology pipelines (ESK-001 TYK2 + izokibep IL-17).
Oral IL-23R peptide (JNJ-2113) discovered by Protagonist, co-developed and commercialised by J&J.
Ex-license of the IL-23 p19 mAb (then MK-3222) to Sun for worldwide development.
Collaboration on next-gen PDE4 inhibition in inflammatory skin disease.

segments.label: Injectable IL-23 (risankizumab, guselkumab, tildrakizumab, ustekinumab originator)
segments.note: Volume growth continues to 2031; risankizumab and guselkumab LOE in the early 2030s drives the ten-year decline. [Estimated]

segments.label: IL-17 (secukinumab, ixekizumab, bimekizumab, brodalumab)
segments.note: Bimekizumab growth roughly offsets secukinumab and ixekizumab maturity; secukinumab LOE early 2030s. [Estimated]

segments.label: Oral targeted systemic (apremilast, deucravacitinib, icotrokinra, zasocitinib, envudeucitinib)
segments.note: Assumes icotrokinra reaches ~$4B and the TYK2 class ~$5B by 2031, and that orals expand the treated pool rather than only switching it. Apremilast generic from Feb 2028. [Estimated]

segments.label: TNF inhibitors and biosimilars (incl. ustekinumab biosimilars)
segments.note: Private-label biosimilar economics compress the whole tier; PBM formulary displacement already underway. [Estimated]

segments.label: Topical and conventional (roflumilast, tapinarof, generic topicals, phototherapy, conventional systemics)
segments.note: Branded non-steroidal topicals take share from generic steroids; paediatric label expansion. [Estimated]

forecast.note: [Estimated] throughout. Base year 2026 = $32.0B, derived as described above. CAGR computed as (end/start)^(1/years) − 1 over the stated horizon.

scenarios.name: Base
scenarios.swing: (1) Advanced-therapy penetration in diagnosed systemic-eligible patients rises from ~45% to ~58%; (2) blended net price erodes ~3%/yr; (3) icotrokinra secures a genuine first-line payer position in year 2; (4) no major LOE inside the window.

scenarios.name: Conservative
scenarios.swing: (1) Orals cannibalise injectables rather than expanding the pool — penetration stalls near 48%; (2) payers position the oral tier behind conventional-systemic AND biologic failure, repeating the Sotyktu experience; (3) net price erodes 6%/yr as three orals compete for the same formulary slot; (4) IRA selection extends to an IL-23 franchise.

scenarios.name: Aggressive
scenarios.swing: (1) Penetration reaches ~70% as oral-first sequencing is adopted in guidelines; (2) adolescent and high-impact-site labels open new segments; (3) net price holds because efficacy tiering is defended on PASI 100 data; (4) a comorbidity-outcomes readout justifies earlier intervention.

segmentation.segment: High-impact site disease (scalp, genital, palmoplantar, nail)
segmentation.why: Severe functional burden at low BSA; systematically excluded by BSA-threshold payer criteria. No dedicated approvals — a live label opportunity.
Small revenue, disproportionate strategic value: first-line habit formation in a lifelong disease. Icotrokinra approved from 12.
Weight-based dosing failure with older agents; worse response rates; the population where a cardiometabolic outcomes claim would be most valuable.

$4,695 vs $13,836 list (−66%). A public reference price for an IL-23 agent — the most consequential single number in the access landscape.
$2,355 vs $7,106 (−67%). Largely rheumatology-relevant but reinforces the reference-pricing precedent.

catalysts_watch.catalyst: Icotyde (icotrokinra) US launch trajectory — first two quarters of scripts and payer coverage
catalysts_watch.why: The single cleanest test of whether an oral with biologic-class efficacy can win a first-line position against rebate-protected injectables. If it repeats Sotyktu's launch curve, the oral thesis is worth materially less.

catalysts_watch.catalyst: Zasocitinib NDA submission and FDA acceptance
catalysts_watch.why: Second next-generation oral into the queue; LATITUDE Atlas showed statistical superiority over deucravacitinib on PASI 100 at week 16, making it the first oral to beat another oral head-to-head.
Third oral entrant; PASI 100 of ~40% at week 24 is competitive. Alumis is the only pure-play, making it the most likely acquisition target in the class.
First head-to-head of an oral against an injectable biologic in plaque psoriasis. A superiority win reframes guideline sequencing.

catalysts_watch.catalyst: Otezla maximum fair price under IRA IPAY 2027
catalysts_watch.why: Sets the Medicare reference price for the oral tier and anchors commercial negotiation for icotrokinra and the TYK2s.
Removes the branded floor from the oral segment and creates a cheap step-therapy rung payers can require before any new oral.

catalysts_watch.catalyst: 2027 PBM formulary decisions on Icotyde and the TYK2 class
catalysts_watch.why: Determines whether the oral tier is positioned first-line or behind biologic failure. This decision, not the clinical data, sets the segment's 2027–2031 revenue.
The single largest determinant of the ten-year forecast; the reason base-case CAGR decays from 7.8% (5y) to 5.3% (10y).

thesis.heading: State of the field
thesis.text: Plaque psoriasis is the most commercially mature autoimmune dermatology indication and, as of 2026, the one undergoing the sharpest structural change in a decade. Two things happened in parallel. First, the injectable ceiling was reached: IL-23 and IL-17 inhibitors deliver PASI 90 in 55–75% of patients and complete clearance in 40–60%, and the marginal gain from the next antibody is small enough that head-to-head superiority trials now turn on PASI 100 rather than PASI 75. Second, on 18 March 2026 the FDA approved icotrokinra (ICOTYDE, Johnson & Johnson) — the first targeted oral peptide antagonist of the IL-23 receptor — for moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis in adults and patients 12 and over weighing at least 40 kg. For the first time an oral agent carries biologic-class efficacy rather than the compromised efficacy of apremilast or, in commercial practice, deucravacitinib. The consequence is that the competitive question has moved from "which molecule clears skin best" to "which molecule gets to the patient first, at what net price". That is a payer question, and the payer environment simultaneously hardened: ustekinumab biosimilars entered the US in February 2025 at discounts up to 85%; the Big Three PBMs removed Humira and Stelara reference products from 2026 formularies in favour of their own private-label biosimilars; and the Inflation Reduction Act set Stelara's maximum fair price at $4,695 versus a $13,836 list from 1 January 2026, with Otezla following in 2027.

thesis.heading: Market size and growth trajectory
thesis.text: We size the global plaque psoriasis therapeutics market at approximately $30B in 2025 and $32B in 2026 [Estimated], built bottom-up from indication-attributable brand revenue and cross-checked against a top-down envelope of $28.9–34.2B from published market-research estimates. Base case takes the market to ~$40B by 2029 and ~$46.5B by 2031 — a 7.8% CAGR over five years — before growth decelerates to a 5.3% ten-year CAGR as risankizumab and secukinumab approach loss of exclusivity in the early 2030s. Growth is almost entirely a mix shift, not a price story: the oral targeted segment grows from ~$3B to ~$14B by 2031 (36% CAGR) while the TNF and biosimilar tier contracts at roughly −15% a year. The headline growth figures circulating in market-research reports — up to $80B by 2034 — are not reconcilable with observed brand revenue and appear to be built on list rather than net prices. Treat them as an upper bound on gross sales, not as a forecast of realisable revenue.

thesis.heading: Competitive landscape highlights
thesis.text: AbbVie is the clear leader: Skyrizi reached $17.56B in FY2025 (+32.5% in Q4 alone) inside a $30.4B immunology portfolio, and did so in only the second full year after the Humira LOE. Johnson & Johnson holds the strongest strategic position for the next cycle — Tremfya grew 40.5% to $5.2B and icotrokinra gives it the first oral IL-23 franchise — but it is simultaneously absorbing a Stelara collapse of roughly 42% in 2025, a ~1,040 basis-point drag on Immunology segment growth. Novartis (Cosentyx, $6.7B) and Lilly (Taltz) are defending mature IL-17 franchises with lifecycle indications rather than new derm efficacy. UCB is the momentum story: Bimzelx reached €2.22B in FY2025, over 200% growth, with 53% of sales from psoriasis. Bristol Myers Squibb is the cautionary tale and the single most instructive data point in this report. Sotyktu, an oral with genuine efficacy and a clean safety profile, reached only ~$0.3B in FY2025 with US revenue flat as higher demand was offset by lower net selling price. BMS's response — routing Sotyktu through a direct-to-patient platform at more than 80% below list from January 2026 — is an admission that the rebate-gated channel would not give an oral a first-line position. Every oral entrant now faces that same wall.

thesis.heading: Key strategic risks and access barriers
thesis.text: The dominant risk is not clinical. It is that the oral era expands the number of competitors without expanding the treated pool — in which case the new entrants cannibalise injectables at a lower net price and the market grows more slowly than the patient count. Step therapy through conventional systemics remains near-universal in the US, and incomplete step documentation is the leading cause of prior-authorisation denial. In Europe, NICE-derived thresholds (PASI ≥10 and DLQI >10) formally exclude most of the moderate segment from biologic access. Meanwhile PBM economics have inverted: private-label biosimilars from Cordavis, Nuvaila and Quallent are displacing both originators and third-party biosimilars, which compresses net price across the whole ustekinumab and adalimumab tier and resets the reference point against which any new oral is priced.

thesis.heading: Investment thesis
thesis.text: Plaque psoriasis remains an attractive market on absolute size and durability but has become a poor market for undifferentiated entry. The value now sits in two places: (1) assets that credibly convert untreated systemic-eligible patients rather than switch already-treated ones — which means oral route, first-line label, adolescent coverage and site-specific data; and (2) capabilities that lower the cost of proving differentiation in an indication where the efficacy bar is already ~70% IGA 0/1 and trials must therefore be large, head-to-head and endpoint-aggressive. A ninth me-too IL-17 or IL-23 antibody has negative expected value. A tenth oral entrant without a payer strategy has the same problem Sotyktu had.

glossary.def: Psoriasis Area and Severity Index — composite 0–72 score of erythema, induration and scaling weighted by body-region area.
Share of patients reaching ≥90% / 100% improvement in PASI from baseline — the modern efficacy bar (PASI 75 was the older standard).
Body Surface Area involved (%); ≥10% (or special sites) marks moderate-to-severe disease.
Dermatology Life Quality Index (0–30) — patient-reported quality-of-life impact; 0/1 means no effect on life.
Dendritic-cell cytokine (p19 + p40 subunits) that sustains pathogenic Th17 cells — the most disease-specific target.
Th17 effector cytokines acting on keratinocytes; blocking them gives the fastest, deepest skin clearance.
IL-23-dependent T-helper subset that produces IL-17 and IL-22.
Intracellular pseudokinase that transduces IL-23 signalling; oral allosteric inhibition (deucravacitinib) avoids the JAK boxed warning.
Kinase→transcription-factor relay beneath cytokine receptors; STAT3 switches on the Th17 programme.
Master transcription factor of Th17 identity; an emerging small-molecule target.
Engineered protein (usually a monoclonal antibody) that neutralises a specific cytokine or receptor; injectable.
Highly similar copy of an off-patent biologic; launches drive steep price competition (Humira, Stelara).
IL-23-specific (p19) vs IL-12/23-shared (p40) subunits; selective p19 blockade proved more effective than p40.
Network meta-analysis — pools many trials to rank therapies never tested head-to-head (e.g. Armstrong 2020).
Real-world persistence: how long patients stay on a therapy before switching/stopping — a durability proxy.
Psoriatic arthritis — inflammatory joint disease developing in ~30% of psoriasis patients.
Patent expiry that opens a product to generic or biosimilar competition.

safety.il23: Cleanest profile — no increased candidiasis or IBD signal (may be IBD-protective), low serious-infection rates, no boxed warnings.
safety.il17: Mucocutaneous Candida (highest with bimekizumab); caution/avoid in IBD; brodalumab carries a boxed warning for suicidal ideation. No consistent MACE/malignancy signal.
safety.tnf: Boxed warnings: serious infections (incl. TB reactivation — screen first) and malignancy (lymphoma). HBV reactivation, demyelination, CHF worsening; highest herpes-zoster rate.
safety.kinase: Selective TYK2 (deucravacitinib) does NOT carry the JAK boxed warning; 5-yr data show no MACE/VTE/malignancy signal. Classic JAK inhibitors carry the class boxed warning (infection, MACE, thrombosis, malignancy).
safety.pde4: No boxed warning, no lab monitoring. GI (diarrhea/nausea), weight loss, and a depression/mood warning (apremilast).
safety.topical: Local, well-tolerated; steroid-sparing. Suitable for chronic use and sensitive sites.
safety.steroid: Skin atrophy, striae, tachyphylaxis and HPA-axis suppression with chronic/high-potency use.
safety.conv: Organ toxicity and monitoring: MTX (hepatotoxicity, cytopenia, teratogenic), cyclosporine (nephrotoxicity, hypertension), acitretin (teratogenic, hyperlipidemia).
